burial.plot_notes: Oldest part of the Milford Cemetery. FindAGrave #29848117.

cross_connections.display_label: Their wives were half-sisters — Fenn family.

narrative_blocks.header: Militia ensign in King Philip's War
narrative_blocks.body: When King Philip's War broke out in 1675, Samuel was appointed ensign in the Milford militia at roughly age 29. He served through the war and its recurring aftermath of Indian skirmishes, rising to captain of the Milford militia by 1698.

narrative_blocks.header: Represented Milford fifteen times in the General Court
narrative_blocks.body: He represented Milford in fifteen sessions of the Connecticut General Court between 1691 and 1703 — a span of sustained civic service that ran alongside his captaincy of the militia. His father Roger Newton Sr. had helped found the town's church.

narrative_blocks.header: Thomas Hooker left him £10 in his will
narrative_blocks.body: He was nine months old when his grandfather Rev. Thomas Hooker died in 1647. Thomas's will left a £10 legacy to him — a substantial inheritance for a Hooker grandchild born during the Hartford founding.

narrative_blocks.header: His wife and cousin's wife were Fenn half-sisters
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Martha Fenn's half-sister Susanna married William Hooker — Samuel's first cousin. The Fenn family thus connected to the Hooker tree twice in the same generation through different lines.

narrative_blocks.header: A second marriage to a soldier's widow
narrative_blocks.body: After Martha Fenn died, Samuel married Sarah Welch Fowler, widow of John Fowler, who had been killed taking an Indian fort in 1693. Their son Abner bore a name found nowhere else in the Newton or Hooker line.

career.notes: Represented Milford in 15 sessions of the General Court, 1691-1703.

military_service.notes: Appointed ensign 1675 at outbreak of King Philip's War. Served in recurring skirmishes with Indians. Rose to captain of Milford militia 1698. Source: Newton Genealogy (Leonard 1915).

burial.plot_notes: Oldest part of the Milford Cemetery. FindAGrave #29848117.

cross_connections.display_label: Their wives were half-sisters — Fenn family.

narrative_blocks.body: When King Philip's War broke out in 1675, Samuel was appointed ensign in the Milford militia at roughly age 29. He served through the war and its recurring aftermath of Indian skirmishes, rising to captain of the Milford militia by 1698.

narrative_blocks.body: He represented Milford in fifteen sessions of the Connecticut General Court between 1691 and 1703 — a span of sustained civic service that ran alongside his captaincy of the militia. His father Roger Newton Sr. had helped found the town's church.

narrative_blocks.header: Thomas Hooker left him £10 in his will
narrative_blocks.body: He was nine months old when his grandfather Rev. Thomas Hooker died in 1647. Thomas's will left a £10 legacy to him — a substantial inheritance for a Hooker grandchild born during the Hartford founding.

narrative_blocks.header: His wife and cousin's wife were Fenn half-sisters
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Martha Fenn's half-sister Susanna married William Hooker — Samuel's first cousin. The Fenn family thus connected to the Hooker tree twice in the same generation through different lines.

narrative_blocks.body: After Martha Fenn died, Samuel married Sarah Welch Fowler, widow of John Fowler, who had been killed taking an Indian fort in 1693. Their son Abner bore a name found nowhere else in the Newton or Hooker line.

parents.mother_research_notes: Sarah Baldwin, bapt. April 22, 1621, Aston Clinton, Buckinghamshire, England
parents.father_research_notes: Benjamin Fenn, b. ca. 1612 Stewkley, Buckinghamshire, England. Emigrated ca. 1630. Assistant Governor of Connecticut 1665-1672. Stewkley is in the same county as Amersham, where Thomas Hooker married Susanna Garbrand on April 3, 1621.

cross_connections.display_label: Her sister married the merchant across from his father's store.

narrative_blocks.header: Her father was Connecticut Assistant Governor
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Benjamin Fenn emigrated from Stewkley, Buckinghamshire, England — the same county where Rev. Thomas Hooker had married Susanna Garbrand in 1621. Fenn served as Assistant Governor of Connecticut from 1665 to 1672. Martha's half-sister Susanna Fenn married William Hooker — bringing the Fenn family into the Hooker tree twice.

cross_connections.display_label: was her father-in-law; Constable of New London 1680

bio.notes: Spelling "Plumbe" used per EH for historical consistency; later generations use "Plumb"

open_questions: Line continues through son David Plumb (b. Stratford June 25, 1751) and further — not entered, pending research.

narrative_blocks.header: Whole family entered the church covenant together
narrative_blocks.body: On April 1, 1711, Samuel and Phebe were admitted to full communion in the Milford church — on the very same day their two eldest children, Phebe and Samuel, were baptized. A single Sunday that sealed the whole family into the covenant at once.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Newton cousins married two Platt sisters
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel married Phebe Platt in Milford; his first cousin Fletcher Newton married Phebe's sister Hannah Platt. Two Newton cousins took two Platt sisters — the families of Milford interlacing through a single generation.

cross_connections.display_label: His first cousin — both married Platt sisters from the same Milford family.

narrative_blocks.header: The whole family entered the church covenant
narrative_blocks.body: On April 1, 1711, she and Samuel were admitted to full communion in the Milford church on the same day their two eldest children, Phebe and Samuel, were baptized. A single Sunday that sealed the whole family into the covenant at once.

narrative_blocks.header: Her sister Hannah married her husband's first cousin
narrative_blocks.body: Phebe married Samuel Newton; her sister Hannah married Samuel's first cousin Fletcher Newton. Two Platt sisters wed two Newton first cousins — the families of Milford knit together across one generation.

cross_connections.display_label: His great-great-grandson married a Hooker descendant — confirmed double Hooker

cross_connections.notes: Their son Jonah Jr. (HD0081) thus has descent_paths_to_thomas unchanged but double Platt ancestry.
cross_connections.display_label: First-cousin marriage to fellow Platt grandchild

narrative_blocks.header: Married his first cousin Phebe Platt
narrative_blocks.body: He married Phebe Platt, his first cousin: his own mother was Phebe Platt and his wife was Phebe Platt, both granddaughters of Joseph Platt of Milford. The two women shared their grandfather, their first name, and their last name.

narrative_blocks.header: Died three days after his wife
narrative_blocks.body: Phebe died April 26, 1756; Jonah died three days later on April 29. Their son Jonah Jr. was about eleven years old. Edward Hooker records both dates in the same entry without comment on the coincidence.

narrative_blocks.header: Tombstone rank 'Sergt.' — daughter went to Vermont
narrative_blocks.body: His tombstone identifies him by the military rank 'Sergt.' — sergeant. His son Jonah Jr. was eleven when he died; his daughter Phebe later married Oliver Sanford and removed to Poultney, Vermont.

narrative_blocks.header: He died at ten years old
narrative_blocks.body: Joseph Newton died on February 20, 1729, at about ten years old.

children_summary.notes: No stars in Edward Hooker despite distinguished career — likely incomplete records for this branch. Birth dates for Caroline Newton children all required correction as given dates predate parents' 1712 marriage.

political_offices.title: Judge, County Court
political_offices.discrepancy_note: EH gives 34 years; Families of Early Milford gives 33 years. Unresolved.

political_offices.discrepancy_note: EH gives 34 years; Families of Early Milford gives 33 years. Unresolved.

sources.notes: Entry 13.5; birth dates for several children required correction

burial.plot_notes: Buried next to wife Susannah. Tombstone inscription preserved below.

narrative_blocks.header: He commanded Connecticut’s troops at Louisbourg
narrative_blocks.body: Commander of Connecticut troops at the Siege of Louisbourg 1745 — capture of French fortress at Cape Breton Island, one of most significant colonial military victories

narrative_blocks.header: Colonel of the Second Connecticut Regiment
narrative_blocks.body: Colonel of the Second Connecticut Regiment

narrative_blocks.header: Chief Judge of County Court 1737-1771 — 34 years
narrative_blocks.body: Chief Judge of County Court 1737-1771 — 34 years

narrative_blocks.header: Assistant of Colony of Connecticut 1738-1771 — 33 years
narrative_blocks.body: Assistant of Colony of Connecticut 1738-1771 — 33 years

narrative_blocks.header: Harvard graduate; Captain in Queen Anne's War 1709
narrative_blocks.body: Harvard graduate; Captain in Queen Anne's War 1709

narrative_blocks.header: “Newton as steel inflexible from right”
narrative_blocks.body: His Milford epitaph closes in verse: “The part the Hero left beneath the skies / Newton as steel inflexible from right / In Faith, in Law, in Equity, in Fight.” He had been thirty-three years a judge and an Assistant of the colony when he died in his eighty-seventh year.

education.notes: Harvard graduate; year not recorded in sources

military_service.role: Commander of Connecticut troops at Siege of Louisbourg
military_service.notes: New England forces captured the French fortress at Cape Breton Island without British regular army support. One of most significant colonial military victories. Many years Colonel of the Second Connecticut Regiment.

bio.title: Colonel
bio.bio_blurb: Louisbourg commander, judge, Assistant thirty-three years

bio.discrepancy_note: Edward Hooker gives primary surname as Bryan; a second account gives Baldwin. Both accounts agree on father Samuel and mother Martha (Whiting). Bryan preferred as primary account.

narrative_blocks.header: Roger died young
narrative_blocks.body: Roger died young; the year was not recorded.

cross_connections.display_label: was her father-in-law

narrative_blocks.header: Married Milford minister, then Middletown statesman
narrative_blocks.body: Her first husband Rev. Samuel Whittlesey served Milford for thirty-plus years until his death in 1768. She remarried Hon. Jabez Hamlin of Middletown — described as 'distinguished in civil and military life.'

narrative_blocks.header: Whittlesey genealogy: 'lady of high intellectual qualities'
narrative_blocks.body: The Whittlesey genealogy describes her as 'a lady of high intellectual and moral qualities.' This was an unusual tribute for a colonial woman, whose lives were typically invisible in family records of the era.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived both husbands; died at 87 in Middletown
narrative_blocks.body: She outlived her first husband Rev. Samuel Whittlesey by 35 years and her second husband Jabez Hamlin as well. She died at Middletown in 1803, age 87.

narrative_blocks.header: Twelve documented children with Chauncey Whittlesey
narrative_blocks.body: Martha Newton Whittlesey and her husband Chauncey Whittlesey had twelve documented children between 1754 and 1773 — a high birth count even for the colonial-era marriages of leading New England families. Of the twelve, five died before reaching adulthood: Elizabeth (born 1758, died 1758), Elizabeth (born 1760, died 1760), John Bryan (born 1761, died 1763), plus Newton (died 1785 at thirty-one) and Susannah (died 1797 at thirty-one).

narrative_blocks.header: Two consecutive daughters named Elizabeth died young
narrative_blocks.body: Martha and Chauncey lost two consecutive daughters both named Elizabeth — one born 1758 died the same year, one born 1760 died the same year. The replacement-naming pattern (same first name given to a later child after an earlier one died young) was characteristic of colonial New England families seeking to preserve a chosen name through high infant mortality. A third Elizabeth, born 1773, finally survived to age ninety-three and never married.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter Susannah married Judge Dyer White
narrative_blocks.body: Martha and Chauncey's daughter Susannah Whittlesey (1766-1797) married Judge Dyer White — connecting the Whittlesey line into the Connecticut bench. The Whittlesey family across this generation produced lawyers, judges, military officers, and merchants spreading across New England.

narrative_blocks.header: Born Milford 1729, lived to age eighty-three
narrative_blocks.body: Martha Newton was born January 27, 1729 in Milford Connecticut, daughter of Colonel Roger Newton, and died October 27, 1812 at age eighty-three. She married Chauncey Whittlesey on August 13, 1753 as his second wife — his first wife Elizabeth Whiting had died October 17, 1751. She survived her husband by many years.

parents.father_research_notes: Thomas Welch of Milford — one of the Seven Pillars of the Milford church

cross_connections.display_label: Their children made a double sibling marriage — son×daughter, daughter×son.

bio.bio_blurb: Milford man who died on Christmas Day, 1711

narrative_blocks.header: He died on Christmas Day at twenty-eight
narrative_blocks.body: Daniel Miles, son of Captain John Miles of New Haven, married Sarah Newton around 1707 and fathered two sons before his death on Christmas Day, 1711, at just twenty-eight. He was buried in New Haven’s Grove Street ground.

narrative_blocks.header: His widow remarried into the Ingersolls
narrative_blocks.body: After Daniel’s early death, Sarah Newton married Jonathan Ingersoll of Milford and had five more children, knitting the Miles and Ingersoll families into the same Hooker-descended line.

parents.father_research_notes: John Ingersoll (ca.1615–Sep 3, 1684) of Westfield, MA. Descended from John Ingersoll Sr. (1626–1684), born Edworth, Bedfordshire, England.

cross_connections.display_label: Their children made a double sibling marriage — son×daughter, daughter×son.

ministry_positions.notes: 40th year of ministry at death per gravestone

notable.notable_blurb: Yale 1736; Pastor of First Congregational Church, Ridgefield…

narrative_blocks.body: Yale 1736; Pastor of First Congregational Church, Ridgefield, CT for 40 years

narrative_blocks.header: Chaplain with Connecticut troops at Fort Ticonderoga 1758
narrative_blocks.body: Chaplain with Connecticut troops at Fort Ticonderoga 1758 under Col. David Wooster

narrative_blocks.header: Preached before the Connecticut General Assembly on Election…
narrative_blocks.body: Preached before the Connecticut General Assembly on Election Day 1761

narrative_blocks.header: In 1777, freed his enslaved man Cyphax (age
narrative_blocks.body: In 1777, freed his enslaved man Cyphax (age 20) with selectmen approval — one of few documented manumissions in Ridgefield

battles: Amherst's campaign 1759 — Fort Carillon to Oswego to St. Lawrence
military_service.notes: Chaplain in Abercromby's ill-fated attack on Fort Carillon 1758. Wrote to a colleague that God showed "distinguishing mercy to the Connecticut Troops" who suffered few deaths in that dreadful slaughter. Following year served under Amherst from Fort Carillon to Oswego and down the St. Lawrence.

narrative_blocks.header: Sarah died young
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah died young; the year was not recorded. Sarah’s parents gave the name to a child born ten years later.

sources.notes: Jeriah Baldwin married Mary Ingersoll bpt Dec 14, 1718, dau of Jonathan and Sarah (Newton)

burial.plot_notes: Buried in Center Church Crypt, New Haven Green. Memorial tablet shared with wife Hannah Whiting Ingersoll.

notable.notable_blurb: Yale graduate 1742; colonial lawyer reaching first eminence…

narrative_blocks.header: Yale graduate 1742; colonial lawyer reaching first eminence…
narrative_blocks.body: Yale graduate 1742; colonial lawyer reaching first eminence at New Haven bar within a dozen years

narrative_blocks.header: Connecticut Stamp Master 1765 — most politically toxic appoi…
narrative_blocks.body: Connecticut Stamp Master 1765 — most politically toxic appointment in the colony; forced to resign by 500 men on horseback at Wethersfield

narrative_blocks.header: Compelled to shout "Liberty and Property" three times
narrative_blocks.body: Compelled to shout "Liberty and Property" three times and throw his hat in the air before being escorted to Hartford under guard

narrative_blocks.header: Judge of Vice-Admiralty Court for the Middle Colonies
narrative_blocks.body: Judge of Vice-Admiralty Court for the Middle Colonies 1770–1777, based in Philadelphia

narrative_blocks.header: Father of Jared Ingersoll Jr.
narrative_blocks.body: Father of Jared Ingersoll Jr. — signer of the U.S. Constitution

education.notes: Remained one year as Berkeley Scholar after graduation

narrative_blocks.header: The Ingersoll and Whiting families exchanged siblings
narrative_blocks.body: She married John Whiting; her brother Jared Ingersoll Sr. married John's sister Hannah Whiting. Two Ingersoll siblings and two Whiting siblings, cross-married in the same generation — the families of New Haven thoroughly interlaced.

parents.mother_research_notes: Sarah Welch Fowler, second wife of Samuel Newton

marriage_date.notes: Married early 1724; deed dated June 18, 1724 gives "Abner Newton of Milford and Mary his wife"

primary_residence.notes: Removed from Milford to Durham, settling there in late 1724. Deed of July 1724 calls him "Abner Newton of Milford"; deed of December 1724 calls him "Abner Newton, late of Milford, now of Durham."

narrative_blocks.header: Half-brother shared same first name, Abner
narrative_blocks.body: This Abner Newton was born 1699 to Samuel Newton's second wife Sarah Welch Fowler — distinguishing him from his half-brother Abner Newton baptized May 1689, who had been born to Samuel's first wife Martha Fenn ten years earlier. The repeated Abner name across both marriages was uncommon. The 1699 Abner became the documented ancestor of the Durham Newton branch — earning the 'durham_newton' tag in this entry.

narrative_blocks.header: 1724 Milford-to-Durham move; wife sold inherited share
narrative_blocks.body: In 1724 Abner Newton and Mary Burwell removed from Milford to Durham Connecticut — and on the move Mary sold her own inherited property, a quarter share of a dwelling house and home-lot in Milford, to Susannah Burwell (widow of John Burwell) for eighty pounds. Per the deed boundaries this was the homestead of the first John Burwell, occupied since 1639. Mary had kept a share in her family's original Milford homestead until the day she left for Durham.

narrative_blocks.header: Son Roger received Alice Newton's Yale bequest
narrative_blocks.body: Abner's son Roger Newton was the beneficiary of Alice Newton's ca.1740 conditional Yale bequest — establishing the family's Yale-funded ministerial track. Roger graduated Yale 1758 and served fifty-six years as minister at Greenfield Massachusetts until 1816 — one of the longest continuous American pastorates of the Revolutionary-Republican era.

narrative_blocks.header: Lexington Alarm minute man at age forty-eight
narrative_blocks.body: He responded to the Lexington Alarm of April 1775 as a minute man in Capt. Jonas Lock's company, Col. Williams's Massachusetts regiment. He had been commissioned earlier to apprehend Tories in Durham, Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Born twin — Greenfield reunion with brother Roger
narrative_blocks.body: He was twin to Abner Newton Jr., born together October 23, 1726 in the Durham Main Street house. After the Revolution he removed to Greenfield, Massachusetts, where his brother Rev. Roger Newton was minister for 53 years.

narrative_blocks.header: Hemlock swamps that became valuable land
narrative_blocks.body: He, his son Isaac, and his brother Roger purchased many acres of wild land in Greenfield at 7 shillings 6 pence per acre. The hemlock swamp later became valuable; he held many civic offices in the town after the war.

narrative_blocks.header: His second wife died two weeks before him
narrative_blocks.body: His second wife Hulda died September 14, 1802 in Greenfield, age 60. He died two weeks later on September 28, age nearly 76.

narrative_blocks.header: Born without a roof to his mouth
narrative_blocks.detail: His principal crop was rye; he spelled it 'ri.' His carefully kept account book, dated January 1, 1762, survived and was published in Caroline Newton's family history. The speech impediment was not only a social burden — it shaped his entire Revolutionary War service. Strangers could not understand him, and when he could not give the countersign intelligibly, he risked being shot by his own side. When Britain declared war with France and the colonial militia faced possible service in Canada, his Captain consulted a higher officer, who granted Burwell an exemption from duty. The document of exemption was preserved in the family. (Caroline Gaylord Newton 1914)
narrative_blocks.body: When the children in the little schoolhouse on Phillida Hill laughed at his cleft-palate speech, Burwell refused to attend. He taught himself to read, write, and keep accounts, his spelling defiantly phonetic — if he chose to spell his principal crop “ri,” that was the best way to spell it.

narrative_blocks.header: Defended Kingston and the Continental Congress in 1777
narrative_blocks.detail: His service in both deployments was brief — not from cowardice but from the practical impossibility of the countersign problem. His unit could not rely on him for sentry duty or night patrol, since any stranger who challenged him could not understand his response. He served with what he could offer and was released. He is on the DAR lineage rolls through his descendant Miss Sarah Newton Church (NSDAR No. 99934; DAR Lineage Book 100:291). (Caroline Gaylord Newton 1914; EH 1909)
narrative_blocks.body: In 1777 Burwell was at Kingston, New York, when the Continental Congress was assembled there under threat from British troops. In 1779 he enlisted in Capt. Charles Norton's Company, Col. Thaddeus Cook's regiment, and was called to New Haven and Fairfield during the British coastal raids of July 1779.

narrative_blocks.header: His stammer nearly kept him from war
narrative_blocks.body: Born without a roof to his mouth, Burwell Newton could not give a military countersign clearly enough for strangers to understand — a danger that could get a sentry shot. When Britain’s war with France threatened to send the militia to Canada, Col. Timothy Stone authorized his discharge, writing that officers had “full Power to Relieve any Soldier… unfit for service.”

cross_connections.display_label: a great-great-grandson who overcame the same speech difficulty

military_service.notes: At the defence of Kingston, New York, 1777, when Continental Congress was assembled there and threatened by British troops. Called to New Haven and Fairfield in July 1779. Did not remain long in service — his speech impediment made it dangerous: strangers could not understand him and he risked being shot when unable to give the countersign intelligibly.

bio.bio_blurb: Durham farmer who soldiered despite a cleft palate

ministry_positions.notes: Ordained November 13, 1761. "Resided at Greenfield, where he was pastor of church for 53 years" per EH (actual span closer to 55 years, 1761-1816).

cross_connections.display_label: She left him a £30 Yale bequest he fulfilled in 1758

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1758 — Greenfield Massachusetts pastor 56 years
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Yale in 1758 and was ordained at the First Church in Greenfield, Massachusetts on November 18, 1761. He served Greenfield for fifty-six years until his death in December 1816.

narrative_blocks.header: Aunt's £30 bequest set the ministry in motion
narrative_blocks.body: His aunt Alice Newton Buckingham left him £30 for college if he proved 'likely for public service' — he was barely three years old when she made the bequest. He graduated Yale eighteen years later and fulfilled her hope.

narrative_blocks.header: Town stayed a single parish all 56 years
narrative_blocks.body: He accepted Greenfield's call on the condition that all persons of 'competent knowledge and sober lives' be permitted communion, avoiding doctrinal narrowness. The entire town remained united in a single parish throughout his ministry — an unusual achievement in the fractious colonial church landscape.

narrative_blocks.header: Baptized an enslaved girl called Tinner in 1767
narrative_blocks.body: In his record of baptisms for September 20, 1767, he noted 'Tinner, my negro girl' — an enslaved person in his household. This stands in tension with his liberal communion policy and the harmony of his ministry.

narrative_blocks.header: Diary: 'I would not live always'
narrative_blocks.body: On May 23, 1812 he wrote in his diary: 'I shall not and I would not live always.' He died four years later in December 1816, in his eightieth year, having outlived his wife by eleven years.

parents.father_research_notes: Said to be of the family of Sir Isaac Newton; possibly son of a Samuel Newton. Parentage unproven — see research_notes.

sources.full: Caroline Gaylord Newton, Rev. Roger Newton, Deceased 1683, and One Line of his Descendants (Durham, Conn., 1914), pp. 3-10
Ermina Newton Leonard, Newton Genealogy (Salem, Mass: Higginson Book Company, 1915), p. 700
Mabel S. Hulburt, Farmington Church and Town (Stonington, Conn: The Pequot Press, 1967), p. 8
William Richard Cutter, New England Families, Genealogical and Memorial (New York: Lewis Historical Publishing, 1913), vol. 3, p. 1730

notable.notable_blurb: Founding Farmington pastor; trained Yale's first rector

narrative_blocks.header: Hooker trained him; he founded Farmington's church
narrative_blocks.body: Roger Newton studied theology at Hartford under Thomas Hooker and, around 1645, married Hooker's eldest daughter, Mary. Ordained the first pastor of Farmington in 1652, he later served twenty-three years as the second pastor of Milford.

narrative_blocks.header: He trained Yale's first rector at his table
narrative_blocks.body: As Hooker had trained him, Newton took young men into his Milford home for theological study. One was Abraham Pierson Jr., who became the first rector of Yale College.

narrative_blocks.header: He sheltered the fugitive regicides near Milford
narrative_blocks.body: From 1661 the regicide judges Edward Whalley and William Goffe — hunted for signing the death warrant of Charles I — hid in a cellar near Newton in Milford. Their presence was kept secret through the two years they remained.

narrative_blocks.header: He brought Tunxis members into the church
narrative_blocks.body: Newton did missionary work among the Tunxis and witnessed the 1650 agreement between Connecticut and the tribe. A few Tunxis became members of his Farmington church, with the standing that membership carried.

narrative_blocks.header: He left Farmington in a year of calamity
narrative_blocks.body: Newton departed Farmington in 1657 after a season of disaster — a man named Scott was murdered, and the house of John Hart burned with most of the family inside. He waited months in Boston for passage to England before settling instead at Milford.

cross_connections.display_label: was received into Milford Church by Newton, 1661
also sheltered the regicides, at Guilford
trained by Newton; became Yale's first rector
succeeded Newton as Farmington's pastor

education.notes: Studied at Harvard but name not on graduate list. Some early Harvard records in which his name would naturally appear were accidentally burned; uncertain he received a diploma. Cotton Mather nevertheless speaks of him as one of the young students who came from England to finish their education in America.

bio.title: Reverend
bio.bio_blurb: First pastor of Farmington; second of Milford

career.notes: Ordained the church's first pastor, October 13, 1652.

burial.plot_notes: Buried in Founders' Cemetery (Rev. Peter Prudden's Garden) on the Wepawaug; 1889 memorial at Founders Memorial Bridge, Milford.
Buried in Peter Prudden's Garden — no tombstone remains

cross_connections.display_label: Her daughter Sarah Newton married her first cousin John Wilson.

narrative_blocks.header: Cared for her mother on the Hartford migration
narrative_blocks.body: According to the Newton family genealogy, Mary was the daughter responsible for caring for her mother Susanna on the arduous journey from Cambridge to Hartford in 1636 — a journey in which Mrs. Hooker 'was carried on a horse litter' in poor health. Mary was roughly 13 years old.

narrative_blocks.header: Stayed in Hartford while Newton built Farmington
narrative_blocks.body: When Roger Newton went to establish the church at Farmington in the late 1640s, Mary remained in Hartford — their son Samuel's baptism on October 20, 1646 appears on the Hartford register, placing her there while her husband laid the groundwork for the congregation he would formally organize.

narrative_blocks.header: Eight children born across three Connecticut towns
narrative_blocks.body: The family's migrations are recorded in their children's birthplaces: Samuel and Roger Jr. born in Hartford; Susannah and John in Farmington; Ezekiel back in Hartford; then Sarah, Mary, and Alice in Milford — where the family finally settled and where Mary died on February 4, 1676.

narrative_blocks.header: Probably born in Esher near Hooker's curacy
narrative_blocks.body: Newton family genealogy says she was born around 1623, probably at Esher, Surrey — where Thomas Hooker had served as curate from c. 1618 to 1625. Esher was also where Hooker met her mother Susanna Garbrand.

burial.plot_notes: Exact location unknown; earliest surviving identifiable gravestone in cemetery dates to 1648 Memorial plaque text: "Thomas Hooker 1586-1647. A leader of the founders in this commonwealth. A preacher of persuasive power. A statesman who based all civil authority on the free consent of the people."

marriages.officiant: Rev. Robert Chaloner, D.D., rector of Amersham (Susanna's aunt's husband)

sources.full: J.R. Hutchinson, 'The Rev. Thomas Hooker—An Alternative Descent,' NY G&B Record 48 (1917), p. 393
Josephine C. Frost, NY G&B Record 64 (Jan 1933), p. 2

narrative_blocks.header: Led 100 settlers from Cambridge to found Hartford
narrative_blocks.body: In the summer of 1636, Hooker led a company of one hundred men, women, and children from Newtown (Cambridge) to found Hartford — a hundred miles through wilderness with no path, guided only by a compass. They drove 160 cattle before them, cleared thickets with axes, built causeways from felled trees, rested in shady groves, and ate wild strawberries. They made the journey in a fortnight.

narrative_blocks.header: His 1638 sermon framed Connecticut's constitution
narrative_blocks.body: On May 31, 1638, preaching before the General Court assembled to frame Connecticut's constitution, Hooker declared: "The foundation of authority is laid in the free consent of the people." The Fundamental Orders of Connecticut, adopted January 14, 1639 — often called the world's first written democratic constitution — grew from this sermon.

narrative_blocks.header: Called the light of the western churches
narrative_blocks.body: Hooker was universally regarded by his contemporaries as the foremost preacher in New England — Cotton Mather called him "the light of the western churches."

narrative_blocks.header: He moderated the Hutchinson trial, then left
narrative_blocks.body: In 1637, Hooker served as one of two moderators over the three-week inquiry into Anne Hutchinson's antinomian doctrines — which culminated in her excommunication and banishment from Massachusetts Bay. Having completed his role, Hooker returned to Hartford before the trial concluded.

narrative_blocks.header: No portrait exists — only his descendants' faces
narrative_blocks.body: There is no known portrait of Thomas Hooker. The statue that stands before the Old State House in Hartford — near the site of the First Meeting House — was sculpted by comparing the likenesses of his descendants.

quotes.text: His genius would still haunt all the pulpits. He had great popularity and influence, especially with young ministers, to whom he was an oracle, and their principle library.

Having gone on, with much strength of voice and intention of spirit, about a quarter of an hour, he was at a stand and told the people, that God had deprived him of both his strength and matter, and so went forth and about half an hour after, returned again, and went on to very good purpose about tw
I giue vnto my sonne Samuell, in case the whole library come not to him... the summe of seuenty pounds.
Brother, I am going to receive mercy!
quotes.attribution: Thomas Hooker, deathbed, July 7, 1647 (Cotton Mather, Magnalia Christi Americana)

career.notes: Cared for Mrs. Joanna Drake; 'had his diet and lodging' in Drake household
Silenced by Bishop Laud for nonconformity
After silencing; John Eliot was assistant

education.notes: B.A. 1608; M.A. 1611; Fellow 1611-c.1618 — Entered divinity course; left before B.D.

narrative_blocks.header: They met in the same household in Surrey
narrative_blocks.body: Susanna Garbrand was working as a waiting-gentlewoman to Mrs. Joanna Drake at Esher, Surrey, when Thomas Hooker arrived in 1620 as curate of Esher church — "having his diet and lodging" in the Drake household. They married at Amersham on April 3, 1621, the ceremony performed by Susanna's own uncle by marriage, Rev. Robert Chaloner.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of Oxford's Reformation bookseller
narrative_blocks.body: Susanna descended from the Garbrands, a Calvinist family who fled the Low Countries for England. Her grandfather Garbrand Harks became the foremost Oxford bookseller of the Reformation and official stationer to the University, a post the family held for three generations.

narrative_blocks.header: Crossed on the Griffin in the great migration
narrative_blocks.body: In 1633 Susanna sailed to New England aboard the Griffin with her husband and children, part of the Puritan migration that brought some twenty thousand settlers before 1640. The family settled first at Cambridge, then helped found Hartford in 1636.

narrative_blocks.header: Remarried Elder William Goodwin after Hooker's death
narrative_blocks.body: After Thomas Hooker died in 1647, Susanna married Elder William Goodwin, a leading Hartford churchman. In 1654 a Hartford court punished Walter Gray for slandering the couple with a charge of adultery.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived Hooker by nearly thirty years
narrative_blocks.body: Susanna outlived Thomas Hooker by almost thirty years, dying at Farmington in 1676 at about eighty-three. Through her eight children the Hooker line descended to figures including the financier J.P. Morgan and President William Howard Taft.

burialCemetery.name: Milford Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes:
Active burial ground for Milford founding families. Mary Hooker Newton (H00003) buried here in what was historically called 'Peter Prudden's Garden' — no tombstone survives for her. John Newton (H00014), Lydia Ford Newton (I00012), Samuel Newton (H00011), and Ezekiel Newton (H00015) also buried here. Prudden was first minister of Milford.
GPS from FindAGrave (H00011 Samuel Newton burial).

crossConnections.display_label: Their wives were half-sisters — Fenn family.
